subject: mathematics
title: Teaching fractions to Class 5 — students are completely lost
query_text: Teaching fractions to Class 5 — students are completely lost

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Start with physical sharing — ask 'If I have 1 roti and 2 children, how much does each get?' before touching the textbook
Group students in pairs and have them fold a paper strip into equal parts — folding IS the fraction
Use local language words alongside English: 'आधा = half = 1/2' written together on the blackboard

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — Paper Folding Fractions
classroom_activity.activity: Give each student one strip of paper (torn from notebook). Ask them to fold it into 2 equal parts — shade one part. Write '1/2' on the board and connect it to what they see. Repeat with 4 equal parts (fold twice) — shade 1, then 3 parts. Ask: 'Which is bigger — 1/4 or 3/4?' Let students hold up their strips and compare visually. Then write 3 fractions on the board and ask pairs to arrange them smallest to largest using their strips.
classroom_activity.materials: One notebook page per student (torn into strips), pencil, blackboard

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: For students who are still struggling, pair them with a peer and ask them only to identify 'how many equal parts total' (denominator concept only). Do not rush to numerator+denominator together — TaRL methodology suggests isolating one concept until it is secure before combining.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After the activity, which students could explain WHY 2/4 and 1/2 look the same on their strip — and what does that tell you about where to go next?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: TaRL (Teaching at the Right Level) Methodology — Pratham/J-PAL India: concept-wise grouping and concrete manipulation before abstraction; NEP 2020 Section 4.14 — bilingual teaching-learning materials to bridge home language and formal concepts; NCERT Math-Magic Grade 4-5 Pedagogical Notes — use of concrete objects before symbolic representation
